beginner.question: What is the <article> element in HTML?
beginner.answer: The <article> element represents a self-contained composition in a document that is independently distributable or reusable, such as a blog post, news story, or comment.

beginner.question: How is <article> different from <section>?
beginner.answer: An <article> is self-contained and could stand alone (e.g., in an RSS feed). A <section> is a thematic grouping that requires its surrounding context to make sense.

beginner.question: Do I need a heading inside every <article>?
beginner.answer: While not strictly required, it's strongly recommended. Headings provide structure, accessibility, and clarity about the article's topic.

beginner.question: Can I put an <article> inside another <article>?
beginner.answer: Yes. This is commonly done for comments on a blog post. The outer article is the blog post, and inner articles are the comments.

beginner.question: What is the default display style of <article>?
beginner.answer:
The
element is a block-level element by default, similar to

.

beginner.question: What kind of content belongs in an <article>?
beginner.answer: Blog posts, news articles, forum posts, user comments, product cards, interactive widgets, and any content that could be independently distributed.

beginner.question: Is <article> a semantic or non-semantic element?
beginner.answer: It's a semantic element. It conveys meaning about the type of content it contains, unlike non-semantic elements like <div>.

beginner.question: Can I use CSS to style an <article>?
beginner.answer: Yes, <article> can be fully styled with CSS like any block-level element using element, class, or ID selectors.

beginner.question: Does the <article> element support global attributes?
beginner.answer: Yes, <article> supports all global HTML attributes including id, class, style, data-*, aria-*, and more.

beginner.question: What does 'self-contained' mean for an <article>?
beginner.answer: Self-contained means the content should make sense even if taken out of context. Someone reading just the article should still understand its message.

intermediate.question: Should I use <article> or <div> for a product card?
intermediate.answer: Use <article> because each product card is self-contained. It could be featured in a comparison tool, wishlist, or email independently.

intermediate.question: What is the role of <header> and <footer> inside an <article>?
intermediate.answer: <header> typically contains the title, author, and publication date. <footer> contains tags, categories, sharing links, and other metadata specific to that article.

intermediate.question: How does the <article> element interact with RSS feed syndication?
intermediate.answer: Content within <article> elements is ideal for RSS syndication because it's self-contained. CMS platforms often extract <article> content for feed generation.

intermediate.question: Can an <article> contain both <header> and <footer>?
intermediate.answer: Yes, an <article> can have its own <header> and <footer>. These are scoped to the article and independent of the page-level header/footer.

intermediate.question: What is the implicit ARIA role of <article> and how does it affect accessibility?
intermediate.answer: The implicit role is 'article'. Screen readers allow users to navigate between article landmarks, making it easier to jump between distinct content items.

intermediate.question: When marking up comments, should each comment be an <article>?
intermediate.answer: Yes, each comment should be an <article> because each comment is a self-contained composition by a user, independent of other comments.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between <article> and <main> in terms of purpose?
intermediate.answer: <main> contains the primary content of the entire page (one per page). <article> represents a self-contained item (multiple per page). <main> can contain multiple <article> elements.

intermediate.question: How should you handle heading levels within nested <article> elements?
intermediate.answer: Maintain proper heading hierarchy. If the outer article uses <h2>, inner articles (comments) should use <h3> to maintain a logical structure.

intermediate.question: Can an <article> be placed directly inside <body>?
intermediate.answer: Yes, an <article> can be a direct child of <body>, though typically it is inside <main> or <section>.

intermediate.question: What is the syndication use case for <article> in a news website?
intermediate.answer: News websites use <article> for each story. Syndication services (Google News, RSS readers) can extract these articles for distribution across platforms.

advanced.question: How does the <article> element relate to the HTML5 document outline?
advanced.answer: <article> is a sectioning element that creates a new entry in the HTML5 document outline. It starts its own sectioning context, similar to <section>.

advanced.question: Explain the difference between <article> and role='article' on a <div>.
advanced.answer: Using <article> provides native semantic meaning and implicit ARIA role. A <div role='article'> provides the same ARIA role but lacks the native semantics, document outline contribution, and default styling.

advanced.question: What are the implications of using <article> in a web component or Shadow DOM?
advanced.answer: In Shadow DOM, each <article> creates its own sectioning context within the shadow tree. The document outline scoping is separate from the light DOM, which can affect accessibility tree composition.

advanced.question: How does the <article> element affect search engine optimization compared to nested <div> elements?
advanced.answer: Search engines use <article> to identify primary content for featured snippets, rich results, and news carousels. Google's algorithms recognize <article> for structured data extraction.

advanced.question: What accessibility issues arise from using <article> without proper heading structure?
advanced.answer: Without headings, screen readers cannot identify the article's topic or navigate between articles efficiently. The article landmark exists but lacks context, reducing its usefulness.

advanced.question: How can you use <article> with structured data (JSON-LD or microdata) for rich snippets?
advanced.answer: You can combine <article> with itemscope and itemtype attributes for schema.org/Article markup, or use JSON-LD in <script> tags. Both help search engines display rich results.

advanced.question: What happens to the document outline when you nest <article> inside <article>?
advanced.answer: Each nested <article> creates a nested sectioning entry. The outer article's outline contains the inner articles, creating a hierarchical structure in the outline algorithm.

advanced.question: How should you handle the <article> element in a print stylesheet?
advanced.answer: In print stylesheets, you might add page-break-before to each <article> to ensure they start on new pages. You may also hide navigation and comments to focus on the main article.

advanced.question: What is the interaction between <article> and the CSS content-visibility property?
advanced.answer: Applying 'content-visibility: auto' to <article> elements can improve performance by deferring rendering of off-screen articles until they are near the viewport.

advanced.question: How do social media platforms use the <article> element for link previews?
advanced.answer: While social media platforms primarily use Open Graph meta tags for previews, the <article> element can help web scrapers identify the primary content for generating link summaries.
